facts.claim: Health officials emphasize the importance of strengthened One Health surveillance and control measures due to the sustained circulation of avian influenza viruses in poultry and wild birds.
The World Health Organization (WHO) monitors avian influenza situations and receives notifications from national authorities regarding human infections with novel influenza A virus subtypes.

There is no evidence of sustained human-to-human transmission of avian influenza A(H5N1); human infections are typically occasional, following exposure to infected animals or contaminated environments.

unsupported_claims: Breaking news from the Associated Press
The World Health Organization announced today that a new variant of the H5N1 avian influenza virus has been detected in three countries across Southeast Asia.
According to Dr. Maria Van Kerkhove, the WHO technical lead on influenza
while the risk to the general public remains low
we are closely monitoring the situation and working with national authorities to ensure rapid containment.
The variant, tentatively named clade 2.3.4.4b.1, shows mutations in the hemagglutinin protein that may affect transmissibility between birds
